highlights.title: Claude Code v2.1.202 Released
highlights.summary: Version 2.1.202 adds a 'Dynamic workflow size' setting in `/config` that lets you control how many agents Claude spins up in dynamic workflows — choose small, medium, or large. It also adds `workflow.run_id` and `workflow.name` OpenTelemetry attributes for better observability in team or CI environments. This is an advisory guideline, not a hard cap, so Claude still uses its judgment.

highlights.title: Claude Code v2.1.200 Released
highlights.summary: This release changes `AskUserQuestion` dialogs so they no longer auto-continue by default — Claude will now wait for your input instead of proceeding on its own, reducing unintended actions. The default permission mode has been renamed from 'default' to 'Manual' across the CLI, VS Code, and JetBrains, making it clearer that you stay in control. Beginners should check their `/config` settings to opt into idle timeouts if they want the old auto-continue behavior.

highlights.title: Claude Code v2.1.199 Released
highlights.summary: You can now stack multiple slash-skill invocations like `/skill-a /skill-b do XYZ` and Claude will load all of them (up to 5), not just the first — great for combining reusable prompts. A long-standing pain point is also fixed: SSL certificate errors from TLS-inspecting proxies or missing `NODE_EXTRA_CA_CERTS` now surface actionable guidance immediately instead of silently burning retries. If you work behind a corporate proxy, this update is worth installing right away.

highlights.title: Claude Code v2.1.198 Released
highlights.summary: Subagents now run in the background by default, so Claude continues working on your main task while subagents execute in parallel and notifies you when they finish — a major speed boost for complex workflows. Claude in Chrome is now generally available, extending agentic coding beyond the terminal. Background agent notifications in `claude agents` make it easy to track which sessions need your input.

highlights.title: Claude Code is Steganographically Marking Requests
highlights.summary: A researcher discovered that Claude Code appears to embed hidden markers in its prompts — a form of steganography — which has sparked debate about transparency and trust in AI coding tools. For developers using Claude Code in sensitive or proprietary environments, this is worth reading to understand what metadata may be embedded in your requests. The discussion highlights the importance of reviewing the tool's behavior before deploying it in security-conscious workflows.

highlights.title: Mastering Claude Code in 30 Minutes
highlights.summary: This YouTube video offers a concise, focused walkthrough of Claude Code designed to get you productive quickly — ideal if you want depth without committing to a multi-hour course. Watching this alongside the recent permission mode changes in v2.1.200 will help you understand how to configure Claude Code safely from the start. It's a great companion resource to the official docs for visual learners.

highlights.title: AI Coding Is a Nightmare. Am I the Only One Experiencing This?
highlights.summary: A popular Hacker News thread surfaces real frustrations with AI coding assistants: duplicate functions, limited context window awareness, and over-confident refactoring that breaks things. The discussion is valuable for Claude Code users because it highlights specific prompting strategies the community has found to mitigate these issues, like providing explicit file scope instructions. Reading through the top comments can help you build better habits early and avoid common pitfalls.

highlights.title: Show HN: Shoaku – Your Coding Navigator (Maintaining Coding Confidence with AI)
highlights.summary: Shoaku is an IntelliJ plugin that addresses a subtle but real problem: developers losing confidence in their own code after relying heavily on AI agents like Claude Code. It acts as a 'coding navigator' to keep you engaged with what the AI is doing, so you understand and own the output. This is a great tool for developers who want to use Claude Code as a collaborator rather than a black box, especially during code reviews.

tips_of_the_day: After updating to v2.1.200+, run `/config` and review your permission mode — the new 'Manual' default means Claude will pause and wait for your approval before taking actions, giving you a safer starting point when working in unfamiliar codebases.

Stack your slash-skills to supercharge prompts: try `/your-style-guide /your-test-template write a new auth module` and Claude Code will load both skills together, letting you enforce multiple conventions in a single command.

If you notice Claude duplicating code or missing context, try explicitly scoping your request — for example, 'Only modify `auth.ts`. Do not create new files.' Tight scope instructions help Claude stay focused and reduce the hallucination of redundant implementations.
